scope: Loss detection: diffing the requirement derivation at a git ref against the working tree to block requirements about to be steamrolled. Belongs here: REQUIREMENT_REMOVED / IMPL_LOST / VERIFY_LOST / SPEC_FILE_DELETED; the supersession and `@spec approve` suppression paths; never-fail-non-git. Boundary case: GOV-01 (an Active req never disappears without approved supersession) folds in here — it names the same concept as GUARD-002 at a second enforcement point (CI/base-ref via check/removed.ts vs pre-commit/working-tree via guard/losses.ts), unified on one diagnostic code (REQUIREMENT_REMOVED). Does not belong here: who may approve a supersession (that is OWNER, the CODEOWNERS policy).

requirements.statement: spec guard [platformDir] [--against <ref>] [--json] compares the requirement derivation at a git ref (default HEAD) against the working tree and reports requirements about to be lost; exit 0 clean, 1 when losses are found, 2 on a usage error.
requirements.why: The derived index has no memory of what a change deletes — git is the memory, so a pre-commit diff of derivations is what catches a requirement being steamrolled.

requirements.statement: spec guard reports REQUIREMENT_REMOVED when a requirement Active at the ref is absent from the working-tree spec with no approved supersession.
requirements.why: Requirements are durable; dropping an Active one without superseding it is exactly the silent loss the derived index cannot feel.

requirements.statement: spec guard reports IMPL_LOST when the last implementing @spec tag for an Active requirement is removed in the working tree.
requirements.why: A requirement that survives in the spec but loses its only implementation has been quietly gutted — the rebuilt index just looks smaller, nothing alarms.

requirements.statement: spec guard reports VERIFY_LOST when the last verifying @spec tag for an Active requirement is removed in the working tree.
requirements.why: Losing the only test that proves a requirement leaves it unguarded while the rebuilt index stays consistent-but-weaker.

requirements.statement: spec guard reports SPEC_FILE_DELETED when a canonical spec file present at the ref is absent from the working tree.
requirements.why: Deleting a whole domain file erases its requirements wholesale; the coarse file-level alarm catches what a scattered per-requirement diff might miss.

requirements.statement: spec guard suppresses a loss when the requirement was properly superseded in the same change (the legal path), in either supersede direction.
requirements.why: Supersession is how requirements legitimately change; the guard must not cry loss when the sanctioned path was followed.

requirements.statement: spec guard suppresses a loss for a requirement when the change carries an explicit @spec approve KEY-NNN <reason> comment — the escape hatch, same UX as biome-ignore.
requirements.why: Sometimes a requirement really should die; an explicit, reasoned, in-diff acknowledgement is the deliberate override — never a silent one.

requirements.statement: spec guard prints a NOT_A_GIT_REPO warning to stderr and exits 0 when the platform is not a git repository or the ref does not resolve — it never fails a non-git context.
requirements.why: The guard is a pre-commit safety net, not a hard dependency on git; a non-git checkout or fresh repo must pass cleanly, not error.

requirements.statement: spec guard --json emits deterministic, chrome-free, byte-stable loss rows on stdout, and the human text output uses the second-person spec-guard block message so an agent can relay it verbatim.
requirements.why: The block message is a product surface agents forward to their user; determinism makes the JSON safe to diff and snapshot.

requirements.statement: A requirement id present in the base ref but absent in the change, without an approved supersession, is a hard error (`REQUIREMENT_REMOVED`) — diff-based, deterministic.
requirements.why: Requirements are durable — silently dropping one without an approved supersession must be a deterministic hard error.
